explanations.4: This checks that both the fzf and docker commands are available on your system, and stops the script if either one is missing.
explanations.5: This checks if there are any running Docker containers, and stops the script early if there aren't any.
explanations.7: This declares several variables that will be used later to store user input and selections from the fzf menu.
explanations.12: This creates a multi-line string that tells Docker how to format and display detailed information about a container when you hover over it in the menu.
explanations.18: The backslash before the dollar signs tells Docker to treat $p and $conf as template variables rather than shell variables.
explanations.19: The {1} at the end means "pass the first column of data (the container ID) from the fzf selection to this preview command".
explanations.31: This extracts just the first line of fzf's output, which tells you which key combination the user pressed (like Ctrl-E or Ctrl-R).
explanations.32: This extracts everything after the first line, which is the actual container information the user selected.
explanations.33: This pulls out just the container ID (the first column) from the selected row using awk, a text-processing tool.
explanations.44: This creates a command that tries to start zsh, falls back to bash if zsh doesn't exist, and finally falls back to sh as a last resort.
explanations.45: This uses tr to remove any extra spaces that might have been added, ensuring the docker command is clean before running it.
explanations.49: Instead of executing the command immediately, this puts it into the zsh input buffer so you can edit it first before pressing Enter.
